title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 2.3 Cross-Sectional, Time Series, and Panel Data
text:
2.3 Cross-Sectional, Time Series, and Panel Data
Before diving deeper, it is worth distinguishing three fundamental types of data structures that economists work with. Understanding these differences will clarify what makes time series analysis a distinct discipline.
Cross-sectional data observe many units — individuals, firms, countries — at a single point in time. The analysis focuses on variation across units, and the standard assumption is that observations are drawn independently from some population. The primary challenges are representativeness and identification: do we have the right sample, and can we isolate causal effects from confounders?
Time series data observe a single unit — or a small number of units — across many points in time. The analysis focuses on variation over time, and the defining feature is temporal dependence: each observation is connected to the ones before and after it. The primary challenges are nonstationarity, structural change, and the fact that we have only one realization of history.
Panel data (also called longitudinal data) combine both dimensions: many units observed across many time periods. A panel dataset might follow 50 US states over 30 years, or 200 countries over 20 years, or 1,000 firms over 10 quarters. Panel data are powerful precisely because they allow researchers to control for unobserved unit-specific characteristics — fixed effects — that would confound a pure cross-sectional analysis.






NoteDefinition 1.2 — Panel Data



A panel dataset is a collection of observations \(\{y_{it}\}\) where \(i = 1, \ldots, N\) indexes units and \(t = 1, \ldots, T\) indexes time periods. The dataset has \(N \times T\) observations in a balanced panel (when every unit is observed in every period) or fewer in an unbalanced panel.


The relationship between panel and time series analysis is close but important to keep straight. In a pure time series setting, we typically have \(N = 1\) and \(T\) large — one long history. In a panel setting, we typically have \(N\) large and \(T\) moderate — many shorter histories. The statistical theory appropriate for each case differs in important ways.
This course focuses on time series analysis: we will work primarily with a small number of series observed over long horizons. Panel methods — fixed effects, random effects, dynamic panel estimators — are covered in econometrics courses and are not our focus here. But it is worth knowing where the boundaries are, and recognizing that many real-world datasets fall somewhere between the two extremes.
The critical difference between panel data and time series data for our purposes is this: in a panel, we can use cross-sectional variation to learn about parameters that would be hard to identify from a single series. In time series analysis, we must extract all information from the temporal variation of one (or a few) series. This constraint shapes everything — what models we use, how we estimate them, and how much we can credibly learn from the data.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
6.1 White Noise
Think of white noise as the absence of pattern in time. It is a sequence of random variables that are mutually uncorrelated, have constant mean, and have constant variance — the residual that remains after all predictable structure in a time series has been accounted for. One way to assess whether a forecasting model is well-specified is precisely to check whether its residuals look like white noise: if they do not, predictable structure remains, and the model is leaving information on the table.






NoteDefinition 1.5 — White Noise



A process \(\{\varepsilon_t\}\) is a white noise process with mean zero and variance \(\sigma^2\) if:

\(\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_t] = 0\) for all \(t\)
\(\text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty\) for all \(t\)
\(\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_t, \varepsilon_s) = 0\) for all \(t \neq s\)

We write \(\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)\).


A white noise process is weakly stationary by construction. Its autocovariance function is:
\[\gamma(h) = \begin{cases} \sigma^2 & h = 0 \\ 0 & h \neq 0 \end{cases}\]
IID noise vs. weak white noise. White noise as defined above imposes only zero correlation — it says nothing about independence. IID noise is the stronger version: \(\varepsilon_t \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} (0, \sigma^2)\), meaning the observations are not just uncorrelated but fully independent and identically distributed. Independence rules out any statistical relationship of any kind, not just linear ones.
The gap between the two matters in practice. It is possible for \(\varepsilon_t\) and \(\varepsilon_{t-1}\) to be uncorrelated but not independent — knowing \(\varepsilon_{t-1}\) tells us nothing about the mean of \(\varepsilon_t\), but may tell us something about its variance. This is precisely the situation in models with conditional heteroskedasticity: residuals are uncorrelated (white noise) but their squared values are correlated, meaning the variance of \(\varepsilon_t\) depends on its own past. Such residuals are weak white noise but not IID noise — a distinction we will revisit when we study volatility models.






TipHierarchy of White Noise



From strongest to weakest:
\[\text{IID noise} \implies \text{Strong white noise} \implies \text{Weak white noise}\]
For most of this course, we work with weak white noise, noting when stronger assumptions are needed.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
6.2 The Autocovariance and Autocorrelation Functions
White noise describes a process with no temporal dependence. But most economic series do have dependence — today’s GDP is correlated with last quarter’s, and inflation this month is correlated with inflation six months ago. To measure and describe this dependence, we need the autocovariance function.
Recall from introductory statistics that the covariance between two random variables \(X\) and \(Y\) measures the degree to which they move together linearly: \(\text{Cov}(X, Y) = \mathbb{E}[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)]\). For a time series, we are interested in how much the series at time \(t\) co-moves with its own past — its value at time \(t - h\).






NoteDefinition 1.6 — Autocovariance Function (ACVF)



For a weakly stationary process \(\{y_t\}\) with mean \(\mu\), the autocovariance function (ACVF) at lag \(h\) is:
\[\gamma(h) = \text{Cov}(y_t, y_{t-h}) = \mathbb{E}[(y_t - \mu)(y_{t-h} - \mu)]\]
By weak stationarity, this quantity depends only on the lag \(h\), not on the time index \(t\).


Three key properties follow immediately from the definition.
Symmetry. \(\gamma(h) = \gamma(-h)\) for all \(h\). The covariance between today and \(h\) periods ago equals the covariance between today and \(h\) periods hence. The ACVF is an even function.
Maximum at zero. \(|\gamma(h)| \leq \gamma(0)\) for all \(h\). The variance of the process — \(\gamma(0) = \sigma^2\) — is always at least as large in absolute value as any autocovariance. This follows from the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality.
Positive semidefiniteness. For any finite collection of lags and real coefficients \(a_1, \ldots, a_k\): \[\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{k} a_i a_j \gamma(t_i - t_j) \geq 0\] This constrains the shape of the ACVF: not every function can be an autocovariance function.
The autocovariance has units — if \(y_t\) is measured in billions of dollars, \(\gamma(h)\) is in billions-of-dollars-squared — which makes it hard to compare across series or across lags. Normalizing by the variance gives a dimensionless quantity.






NoteDefinition 1.7 — Autocorrelation Function (ACF)



For a weakly stationary process \(\{y_t\}\), the autocorrelation function (ACF) at lag \(h\) is:
\[\rho(h) = \frac{\gamma(h)}{\gamma(0)} = \frac{\text{Cov}(y_t, y_{t-h})}{\text{Var}(y_t)}\]
By construction, \(\rho(0) = 1\) and \(|\rho(h)| \leq 1\) for all \(h\).


The ACF is our primary graphical diagnostic tool. An ACF plot shows \(\hat{\rho}(h)\) — the sample estimate of \(\rho(h)\) — for \(h = 1, 2, \ldots, H\). The shape of the ACF reveals the temporal structure of the series. A slowly decaying ACF suggests a trending or persistent series; an ACF that drops sharply to zero after a few lags suggests a process with short memory; an ACF that is zero at all nonzero lags is the signature of white noise.
We estimate \(\gamma(h)\) from the data using the sample autocovariance:
\[\hat{\gamma}(h) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=h+1}^{T} (y_t - \bar{y})(y_{t-h} - \bar{y})\]
Note the divisor is \(T\), not \(T - h\), even though only \(T - h\) terms appear in the sum. Using \(T\) ensures positive semidefiniteness of the sample ACVF matrix. The sample ACF is \(\hat{\rho}(h) = \hat{\gamma}(h) / \hat{\gamma}(0)\).
Under the null hypothesis that \(\{y_t\}\) is white noise, sample autocorrelations are approximately normally distributed with mean zero and variance \(1/T\) for large \(T\), giving the standard 95% confidence bands displayed in ACF plots:
\[\pm \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{T}}\]






WarningA Caution on ACF Interpretation



The confidence bands assume the true process is white noise. They are appropriate for testing whether a series has any autocorrelation, but when the ACF shows a clear pattern — slow decay, oscillation, seasonal spikes — interpret the shape as a whole rather than testing each lag individually.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
6.3 The Partial Autocorrelation Function
The ACF has an important limitation when we want to understand the direct dependence between \(y_t\) and \(y_{t-h}\). Because observations at neighboring lags are themselves correlated, the ACF at lag \(h\) picks up both the direct effect of \(y_{t-h}\) on \(y_t\) and the indirect effects transmitted through intermediate lags \(y_{t-1}, \ldots, y_{t-h+1}\).
To see why this matters, consider a process where \(y_t\) depends directly only on \(y_{t-1}\): \(y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t\). Because \(y_{t-1}\) is correlated with \(y_{t-2}\), and \(y_{t-2}\) with \(y_{t-3}\), the ACF will show nonzero correlations at all lags — not just lag 1. The ACF alone does not tell us how many lags directly matter.
The partial autocorrelation function (PACF) solves this by isolating the direct relationship. It asks: once we account for the intermediate observations \(y_{t-1}, \ldots, y_{t-h+1}\), how much additional correlation remains between \(y_t\) and \(y_{t-h}\)?
The answer comes from partial regression. The partial autocorrelation at lag \(h\), denoted \(\alpha(h)\), is the coefficient on \(y_{t-h}\) in the population OLS regression of \(y_t\) on its \(h\) most recent lags:
\[y_t = \phi_{h,1} y_{t-1} + \phi_{h,2} y_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_{h,h} y_{t-h} + \varepsilon_t^{(h)}\]
The PACF at lag \(h\) is the last coefficient: \(\alpha(h) = \phi_{h,h}\). It measures the marginal contribution of \(y_{t-h}\) to predicting \(y_t\), after all shorter lags have already been included.
The first two lags are instructive. At lag 1, there are no intermediate observations to control for, so the PACF equals the ordinary autocorrelation: \(\alpha(1) = \rho(1)\). At lag 2, after removing the linear effect of \(y_{t-1}\) from both \(y_t\) and \(y_{t-2}\):
\[\alpha(2) = \frac{\rho(2) - \rho(1)^2}{1 - \rho(1)^2}\]
For general \(h\), the partial autocorrelations are computed recursively via the Yule-Walker equations:
\[\alpha(h) = \frac{\rho(h) - \sum_{j=1}^{h-1} \phi_{h-1,j}\,\rho(h-j)}{1 - \sum_{j=1}^{h-1}\phi_{h-1,j}\,\rho(j)}\]
In practice, software handles this recursion automatically.






NoteDefinition 1.8 — Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF)



The partial autocorrelation function (PACF) at lag \(h\) is the coefficient \(\alpha(h) = \phi_{h,h}\) on \(y_{t-h}\) in the population OLS regression of \(y_t\) on \((y_{t-1}, \ldots, y_{t-h})\). It measures the linear dependence between \(y_t\) and \(y_{t-h}\) after removing the linear influence of all intermediate lags.


The ACF and PACF together will become our primary tools for identifying the structure of time series models in later chapters. A process whose ACF decays slowly but whose PACF drops sharply after a small number of lags has a very different structure from one where both functions decay gradually. We will develop these identification patterns carefully when we introduce specific model classes in Chapter 2.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 6.4 Python Example: White Noise in Practice
text:
6.4 Python Example: White Noise in Practice
Before moving on, it is instructive to see what white noise actually looks like — and to appreciate that “white noise” does not mean the series is uninteresting or well-behaved in every respect. The code below simulates three versions: IID Gaussian, IID Student-\(t\) with heavy tails, and an ARCH(1) process with conditional heteroskedasticity.
All three are white noise by our definition: zero mean, constant variance, zero autocovariances at all nonzero lags. But they look and behave quite differently in practice.


Show code — Figure 1.1
rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=42)
T   = 300

# IID Gaussian white noise
eps_gaussian = rng.normal(loc=0, scale=1, size=T)

# IID Student-t (df=3), rescaled to unit variance
eps_student  = rng.standard_t(df=3, size=T)
eps_student  = eps_student / eps_student.std()

# ARCH(1): sigma_t^2 = 0.2 + 0.75 * eps_{t-1}^2
alpha0, alpha1 = 0.2, 0.75
eps_arch       = np.zeros(T)
sigma2         = np.zeros(T)
sigma2[0]      = alpha0 / (1 - alpha1)
for t in range(1, T):
    sigma2[t]   = alpha0 + alpha1 * eps_arch[t - 1] ** 2
    eps_arch[t] = np.sqrt(sigma2[t]) * rng.normal()

series = [eps_gaussian, eps_student, eps_arch]
labels = ["IID Gaussian", "IID Student-$t$ (df=3)", "ARCH(1)"]
colors = [EO_COPPER, EO_SKYBLUE, EO_SAGE]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 2, figsize=(7, 9))

for i, (y, label, color) in enumerate(zip(series, labels, colors)):
    # Time plot
    ax = axes[i, 0]
    ax.plot(y, color=color, linewidth=0.8, alpha=0.9)
    ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.5, linestyle="--")
    ax.set_title(label)
    ax.set_xlabel("Time")
    ax.set_ylabel("Value")
    eo_style_ax(ax)

    # ACF
    ax = axes[i, 1]
    plot_acf(y, lags=30, ax=ax, color=color,
             vlines_kwargs={"colors": color},
             title="", zero=False, alpha=0.05)
    ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.5)
    ax.set_title(f"ACF — {label}")
    ax.set_xlabel("Lag")
    ax.set_ylabel("ACF")
    eo_style_ax(ax)
    for line in ax.lines:
        if line.get_linestyle() == "--":
            line.set_color(EO_TERRACOTTA)
            line.set_linewidth(0.8)

eo_suptitle(fig, "Three Flavors of White Noise")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 6.1: Three versions of white noise. Each row shows one series: its time plot (left) and sample ACF (right). All three have statistically zero autocorrelations at all lags — confirming they are white noise. The ARCH(1) series in the bottom row shows visible volatility clustering in its time plot: stretches of large and small fluctuations that reveal real structure in the second moment, invisible to the ACF.





Notice two things. First, all three ACF plots show no significant spikes at lags \(h \geq 1\) — the autocorrelations are statistically indistinguishable from zero, confirming that all three are white noise by our definition. Second, the ARCH(1) time plot clearly shows volatility clustering — stretches of large fluctuations and stretches of smaller ones. This structure is real and economically important, but it lives in the second moment (variance), not the first moment (mean), so it is invisible to the ACF. This illustrates precisely why the distinction between IID noise and weak white noise matters in practice.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
11.2 The Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

The Regression Framework
The ADF test builds directly on the AR(1) regression framework derived in Section 7. Recall that \(y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + c + \varepsilon_t\) can be rewritten as:
\[\Delta y_t = \delta y_{t-1} + c + \varepsilon_t, \qquad \delta = \phi - 1\]
The unit root null \(\phi = 1\) becomes \(H_0: \delta = 0\); the stationary alternative \(\phi < 1\) becomes \(H_1: \delta < 0\). This is a one-sided test. The Augmented Dickey-Fuller test adds lagged differences to absorb serial correlation in the errors:
\[\Delta y_t = \delta y_{t-1} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \psi_j \Delta y_{t-j} + \alpha + \beta t + u_t \tag{1.7}\]
The test statistic is the \(t\)-ratio on \(\hat{\delta}\). Three specifications correspond to different assumptions about deterministic components:



Table 11.1: ADF specification choices










Specification
Deterministic terms
Use when…




No constant, no trend
None
Series is zero-mean with no drift


Constant only
\(\alpha\)
Series has a non-zero mean


Constant and trend
\(\alpha + \beta t\)
Series may trend under \(H_1\)






Always inspect the time plot before choosing. Including a trend when none is present reduces power; omitting a constant when one exists distorts size.


Non-Standard Critical Values
Under \(H_0\), \(y_{t-1}\) is an integrated process, so \(\hat{\delta}\) does not follow a standard \(t\)-distribution — it follows the left-skewed Dickey-Fuller distribution, with more mass in its left tail than the normal. Using standard critical values would cause severe under-rejection of the unit root null. The correct critical values were tabulated by Dickey and Fuller (1979) and are built into all standard software.


Choosing the Lag Length
Information criteria. Before covering information criteria formally — that requires introducing likelihood, which we do in a later chapter — the basic idea is intuitive. More lags always improve in-sample fit, but adding lags that carry no real information makes the test noisier. Information criteria formalize this tradeoff by penalizing complexity. The AIC and BIC take the form “measure of fit minus penalty for complexity”:
\[\text{AIC}(p) = \log\hat{\sigma}^2_p + \frac{2(p+k)}{T} \qquad \text{BIC}(p) = \log\hat{\sigma}^2_p + \frac{\log(T)(p+k)}{T}\]
where \(\hat{\sigma}^2_p\) is the estimated residual variance and \(k\) counts the deterministic terms. BIC penalizes additional lags more heavily than AIC and tends to select more parsimonious models. We select the \(p\) that minimizes the chosen criterion. The full technical treatment — why these specific penalty terms, how they relate to likelihood, and their asymptotic properties — is developed in the model selection chapter.
General-to-specific (GETS). Start with \(p_{\max} = \lfloor 12(T/100)^{1/4} \rfloor\) (the Schwert rule). Test whether the last lag \(\psi_p\) is statistically significant. If not, drop it and test \(\psi_{p-1}\), continuing until a significant lag is found or \(p = 0\).


Interpreting the Output
The ADF test produces three things: a test statistic (always negative; more negative means stronger evidence against the unit root null), a p-value (below 0.05 leads to rejection at the 5% level), and critical values at 1%, 5%, and 10%.






WarningTwo Common Mistakes



Mistake 1: Failing to reject is not evidence of a unit root. The ADF has low power against near-unit-root alternatives. A large p-value means the data are insufficient to reject the null, not that the null is true.
Mistake 2: The specification matters. Always let the visual appearance of the data guide the choice of deterministic terms.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
11.3 The KPSS Test

Reversing the Null
The ADF test is conservative about rejecting unit roots — by design, since it controls the probability of falsely declaring a unit root series stationary. A near-unit-root series may routinely survive the ADF test. The KPSS test (Kwiatkowski, Phillips, Schmidt, and Shin, 1992) addresses this by reversing the null: stationarity is the null, a unit root is the alternative. Running both tests together provides evidence that neither test alone can supply.


The Regression Framework
The KPSS test decomposes the series as:
\[y_t = \xi t + r_t + \varepsilon_t\]
where \(\xi t\) is a deterministic trend, \(\varepsilon_t\) is a stationary error, and \(r_t = r_{t-1} + u_t\) is a random walk with \(u_t \sim WN(0,\sigma_u^2)\). The null \(\sigma_u^2 = 0\) means the random walk component is absent — the series is stationary around its deterministic part. The alternative \(\sigma_u^2 > 0\) allows a unit root.


The Test Statistic
Let \(\hat{\varepsilon}_t\) be OLS residuals from regressing \(y_t\) on the deterministic terms, and define partial sums \(S_t = \sum_{s=1}^t \hat{\varepsilon}_s\). The KPSS statistic is:
\[\text{KPSS} = \frac{1}{T^2}\,\frac{\sum_{t=1}^T S_t^2}{\hat{\lambda}^2}\]
where \(\hat{\lambda}^2\) is a long-run variance estimator that corrects for serial correlation in \(\hat{\varepsilon}_t\), using a weighted sum of sample autocovariances with weights that downweight distant lags. The idea parallels the augmentation lags in the ADF test: ignoring serial correlation would distort the test statistic. The bandwidth controlling how many lags are included is chosen automatically by most software — a common rule is \(l = \lfloor 4(T/100)^{1/4} \rfloor\) — and results should be checked for sensitivity to this choice.


Critical Values and Interpretation



Table 11.2: KPSS asymptotic critical values





Specification
10%
5%
1%




Level stationary (constant only)
0.347
0.463
0.739


Trend stationary (constant + trend)
0.119
0.146
0.216






The rejection rule is reversed relative to the ADF. A large KPSS statistic leads to rejection of the stationarity null — large values indicate that cumulative residuals \(S_t\) drift systematically, suggesting nonstationarity.


Reading ADF and KPSS Jointly



Table 11.3: Joint interpretation of ADF and KPSS results










ADF
KPSS
Joint conclusion




Reject \(H_0\)
Fail to reject \(H_0\)
Both agree: stationary


Fail to reject \(H_0\)
Reject \(H_0\)
Both agree: unit root


Reject \(H_0\)
Reject \(H_0\)
Contradictory: near-integrated or structural break


Fail to reject \(H_0\)
Fail to reject \(H_0\)
Contradictory: insufficient information






When the tests agree, the conclusion is on solid ground. When they disagree, further investigation is warranted — subsamples, structural break tests, or economic reasoning about the likely behavior of the series.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
7.4 Achieving Stationarity: Transformations
When a series is found to be nonstationary, the goal is to apply a transformation that removes the source of nonstationarity while preserving the economically meaningful variation. The two most common transformations are differencing and logarithms, which are often applied together.

First Differencing
Taking first differences removes a stochastic trend. If \(y_t\) is nonstationary but \(\Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}\) is stationary, we say \(y_t\) is integrated of order one, written \(y_t \sim I(1)\). If \(y_t\) must be differenced \(d\) times to achieve stationarity, it is \(I(d)\).
The economic interpretation of first differencing depends on the series. For a price level, \(\Delta y_t\) is the change in prices — inflation. For a nominal exchange rate, \(\Delta y_t\) is the change in the exchange rate. For a log series (see below), \(\Delta y_t\) is the approximate percentage change.
Most economic level series are \(I(1)\): GDP, price levels, exchange rates, asset prices. Their first differences are \(I(0)\): growth rates, inflation rates, returns. This is one of the most important empirical regularities in macroeconometrics.


Logarithms
Taking the natural log of a series with an exponential trend linearizes that trend, making it easier to remove by differencing. For a level series \(Y_t\), we define \(y_t = \log Y_t\). Then:
\[\Delta y_t = \log Y_t - \log Y_{t-1} = \log\!\left(\frac{Y_t}{Y_{t-1}}\right) \approx \frac{Y_t - Y_{t-1}}{Y_{t-1}}\]
the last approximation holding for small changes. This means log-differencing gives us the approximate percentage change — a natural and interpretable unit for growth rates, inflation, and returns.
The log transformation also stabilizes variance. Series that grow exponentially tend to have variance proportional to their level — a 1% change in a large GDP figure produces a much larger absolute change than a 1% change in a small one. Taking logs removes this level-dependence, making the series (closer to) homoskedastic.


Choosing the Right Transformation
The choice of transformation should be guided by three considerations:
Economic interpretability. Log differences give percentage changes, which are natural units for most macroeconomic and financial series. Level differences give absolute changes, which may be more natural for series measured in rates (interest rates, unemployment rates).
Visual inspection. Plot the level series and the transformed series. If the log-level series has a roughly linear trend and the log-differenced series fluctuates around a stable mean, the transformation is working well.
Formal testing. Apply ADF and KPSS to both the level and the transformed series. If the level series fails to reject the unit root (ADF) and rejects stationarity (KPSS), while the differenced series rejects the unit root and fails to reject stationarity, the transformation has successfully achieved stationarity.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
7.5 Python Example: Real Data Analysis
We now put all the tools developed in this chapter to work on two series from the Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) database:

GDPC1: US Real GDP, chained 2017 dollars, quarterly, seasonally adjusted.
CPIAUCSL: US CPI for All Urban Consumers, monthly, seasonally adjusted.



Show code — Data preview
print("Real GDP (last 5 observations):")
print(gdp[["Real GDP", "GDP Growth (QoQ)"]].tail().round(3).to_string())
print(f"\n{gdp.shape[0]} quarterly observations, "
      f"{gdp.index[0].date()} to {gdp.index[-1].date()}")

print("\nCPI (last 5 observations):")
print(cpi[["CPI", "Inflation (YoY)"]].tail().round(3).to_string())
print(f"\n{cpi.shape[0]} monthly observations, "
      f"{cpi.index[0].date()} to {cpi.index[-1].date()}")


Real GDP (last 5 observations):
             Real GDP  GDP Growth (QoQ)
DATE                                   
2023-04-01  22580.499             0.626
2023-07-01  22840.989             1.147
2023-10-01  23033.780             0.841
2024-01-01  23082.119             0.210
2024-04-01  23286.508             0.882

258 quarterly observations, 1960-01-01 to 2024-04-01

CPI (last 5 observations):
                CPI  Inflation (YoY)
DATE                                
2024-02-01  310.967            3.108
2024-03-01  312.345            3.427
2024-04-01  313.023            3.306
2024-05-01  313.175            3.193
2024-06-01  313.044            2.927

774 monthly observations, 1960-01-01 to 2024-06-01



Time Plots: Levels and Transformed Series
The first thing we always do with a new time series is plot it. A well-constructed time plot reveals trend, structural breaks, outliers, and changing volatility — features that inform every subsequent modeling decision.


Show code — Figure 1.2
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(7, 7))

# GDP Level
ax = axes[0, 0]
ax.plot(gdp.index, gdp["Real GDP"], color=EO_COPPER, linewidth=1.2)
ax.set_title("Real GDP — Level")
ax.set_ylabel("Billions (2017 $)")
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(
    mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${x/1000:.0f}T"))
eo_style_ax(ax)

# GDP Growth
ax = axes[0, 1]
gdp_growth = gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"].dropna()
ax.fill_between(gdp_growth.index, gdp_growth, 0,
                where=gdp_growth >= 0,
                color=EO_SAGE, alpha=0.7, label="Positive")
ax.fill_between(gdp_growth.index, gdp_growth, 0,
                where=gdp_growth < 0,
                color=EO_TERRACOTTA, alpha=0.7, label="Negative")
ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.6, linestyle="--")
ax.set_title("Real GDP — QoQ Growth (%)")
ax.set_ylabel("Percent")
ax.legend(fontsize=6)
eo_style_ax(ax)

# CPI Level
ax = axes[1, 0]
ax.plot(cpi.index, cpi["CPI"], color=EO_SKYBLUE, linewidth=1.2)
ax.set_title("CPI — Level")
ax.set_ylabel("Index (1982–84 = 100)")
eo_style_ax(ax)

# CPI Inflation
ax = axes[1, 1]
inf_series = cpi["Inflation (YoY)"].dropna()
ax.plot(inf_series.index, inf_series, color=EO_SKYBLUE, linewidth=1.0)
ax.axhline(inf_series.mean(), color=EO_COPPER, linewidth=0.8,
           linestyle="--", label=f"Mean = {inf_series.mean():.1f}%")
ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.4, linestyle=":")
ax.set_title("CPI — Year-on-Year Inflation (%)")
ax.set_ylabel("Percent")
ax.legend(fontsize=6)
eo_style_ax(ax)

for ax in axes.flatten():
    ax.set_xlabel("")

eo_suptitle(fig, "US Real GDP and CPI, 1960–2024")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 7.1: US Real GDP and CPI, 1960–2024. Left column: level series. Right column: transformed series (QoQ growth rate for GDP; year-on-year inflation for CPI). The level series are clearly nonstationary — both trend persistently upward with no stable mean. The transformed series are much closer to stationarity, though changing volatility is visible in both. The COVID-19 contraction in Q2 2020 is the most extreme observation in the GDP growth series.





The level series tell a clear story: both GDP and CPI trend persistently upward. No fixed mean exists; variances are not constant. These are nonstationary series. The transformed series in the right column look much closer to stationary fluctuations around a stable mean — though even here, the inflation series shows periods of higher and lower volatility, suggesting the stationarity assumption deserves formal scrutiny.


ACF and PACF Analysis
We now examine the autocorrelation structure of each series. The contrast between the level and transformed series is the clearest possible illustration of the theory developed in this chapter.


Show code — Figure 1.3
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(7, 7))

plot_acf(gdp["Log Real GDP"].dropna(), lags=20, ax=axes[0, 0],
         color=EO_COPPER, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_COPPER},
         title="ACF — Log Real GDP (Level)", zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_pacf(gdp["Log Real GDP"].dropna(), lags=20, ax=axes[0, 1],
          color=EO_COPPER, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_COPPER},
          method="ywm", title="PACF — Log Real GDP (Level)",
          zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_acf(gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"].dropna(), lags=20, ax=axes[1, 0],
         color=EO_SAGE, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_SAGE},
         title="ACF — GDP Growth Rate", zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_pacf(gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"].dropna(), lags=20, ax=axes[1, 1],
          color=EO_SAGE, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_SAGE},
          method="ywm", title="PACF — GDP Growth Rate",
          zero=False, alpha=0.05)

for ax in axes.flatten():
    ax.set_xlabel("Lag")
    ax.set_ylabel("Correlation")
    eo_style_ax(ax)
    for line in ax.lines:
        if line.get_linestyle() == "--":
            line.set_color(EO_TERRACOTTA)
            line.set_linewidth(0.8)

eo_suptitle(fig, "ACF and PACF: US Real GDP")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 7.2: ACF and PACF for US Real GDP. Top row: log level. Bottom row: QoQ growth rate. The log-level ACF decays extremely slowly — barely moving from 1.0 across many lags — the unmistakable visual fingerprint of a nonstationary, integrated process. After differencing, the ACF of GDP growth drops sharply and the PACF shows one dominant spike at lag 1, suggesting a short-memory process.







Show code — Figure 1.4
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(7, 7))

plot_acf(cpi["Log CPI"].dropna(), lags=24, ax=axes[0, 0],
         color=EO_SKYBLUE, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_SKYBLUE},
         title="ACF — Log CPI (Level)", zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_pacf(cpi["Log CPI"].dropna(), lags=24, ax=axes[0, 1],
          color=EO_SKYBLUE, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_SKYBLUE},
          method="ywm", title="PACF — Log CPI (Level)",
          zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_acf(cpi["Inflation (YoY)"].dropna(), lags=24, ax=axes[1, 0],
         color=EO_LAVENDER, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_LAVENDER},
         title="ACF — Year-on-Year Inflation", zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_pacf(cpi["Inflation (YoY)"].dropna(), lags=24, ax=axes[1, 1],
          color=EO_LAVENDER, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_LAVENDER},
          method="ywm", title="PACF — Year-on-Year Inflation",
          zero=False, alpha=0.05)

for ax in axes.flatten():
    ax.set_xlabel("Lag")
    ax.set_ylabel("Correlation")
    eo_style_ax(ax)
    for line in ax.lines:
        if line.get_linestyle() == "--":
            line.set_color(EO_TERRACOTTA)
            line.set_linewidth(0.8)

eo_suptitle(fig, "ACF and PACF: US CPI")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 7.3: ACF and PACF for US CPI. Top row: log level. Bottom row: year-on-year inflation. The log-level CPI has a near-unit ACF — the unmistakable signature of nonstationarity. The inflation series has a more complex autocorrelation structure: the ACF decays slowly and remains significant at many lags, while the PACF drops sharply after the first two lags. This suggests richer dynamics than simple white noise, which we will model formally in later chapters.





The slowly decaying ACF of the log-level series is the visual fingerprint of a unit root. After transformation, the ACF drops quickly — though for inflation, significant autocorrelation persists at many lags, pointing to dynamics we will model formally in Chapter 2.


Formal Stationarity Tests
Visual inspection and ACF plots provide strong intuitions. We now formalize them with ADF and KPSS tests across all four series. For level series we use the constant-and-trend specification, since both trend upward under the stationary alternative. For transformed series we use constant only, since both fluctuate around a stable non-zero mean.


Show code — ADF and KPSS tests
def run_tests(series, name, adf_reg="ct", kpss_reg="ct"):
    """Run ADF and KPSS tests and print formatted results."""
    clean = series.dropna()

    # ── ADF ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    adf_res  = adfuller(clean, regression=adf_reg, autolag="AIC")
    adf_stat = adf_res[0]
    adf_p    = adf_res[1]
    adf_lags = adf_res[2]
    adf_cv   = adf_res[4]
    adf_conc = ("Reject H\u2080 \u2192 Stationary"
                if adf_p < 0.05 else "Fail to reject H\u2080 \u2192 Unit root")

    # ── KPSS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    kpss_res  = kpss(clean, regression=kpss_reg, nlags="auto")
    kpss_stat = kpss_res[0]
    kpss_p    = kpss_res[1]
    kpss_lags = kpss_res[2]
    kpss_cv   = kpss_res[3]
    kpss_conc = ("Reject H\u2080 \u2192 Unit root"
                 if kpss_p < 0.05 else "Fail to reject H\u2080 \u2192 Stationary")

    print(f"\n{'═'*55}")
    print(f"  Series: {name}")
    print(f"{'═'*55}")

    print(f"\n  ADF Test  (H\u2080: unit root | spec: '{adf_reg}')")
    print(f"  {'─'*50}")
    print(f"  Test statistic   : {adf_stat:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  p-value          : {adf_p:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Lags (AIC)       : {adf_lags:>9d}")
    for lv, cv in adf_cv.items():
        print(f"  Critical val {lv:>4s}: {cv:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Conclusion       : {adf_conc}")

    print(f"\n  KPSS Test (H\u2080: stationary | spec: '{kpss_reg}')")
    print(f"  {'─'*50}")
    print(f"  Test statistic   : {kpss_stat:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  p-value          : {kpss_p:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Lags (bandwidth) : {kpss_lags:>9d}")
    for lv, cv in kpss_cv.items():
        print(f"  Critical val {lv:>4s}: {cv:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Conclusion       : {kpss_conc}")
    print(f"{'═'*55}")

run_tests(gdp["Log Real GDP"],      "Log Real GDP (Level)",
          adf_reg="ct", kpss_reg="ct")
run_tests(gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"],  "GDP QoQ Growth Rate",
          adf_reg="c",  kpss_reg="c")
run_tests(cpi["Log CPI"],           "Log CPI (Level)",
          adf_reg="ct", kpss_reg="ct")
run_tests(cpi["Inflation (YoY)"],   "Year-on-Year Inflation",
          adf_reg="c",  kpss_reg="c")

  KPSS Test (H₀: stationary | spec: 'c')
  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Test statistic   :    0.8611
  p-value          :    0.0100

Reading the results carefully:
Log Real GDP and Log CPI. Both tests agree: the ADF fails to reject the unit root null (large p-value, test statistic close to zero) and the KPSS rejects the stationarity null (statistic exceeds the critical value). The two tests reinforce each other — both level series have unit roots. They are \(I(1)\) processes.
GDP Growth Rate. Again both tests agree, in the opposite direction: the ADF rejects the unit root null decisively (strongly negative statistic, p-value near zero) and the KPSS fails to reject stationarity. GDP growth is \(I(0)\) — stationary. Log-differencing the level series has successfully achieved stationarity.
Year-on-Year Inflation. The results are more nuanced. The ADF rejects the unit root at the 5% level, but the KPSS result deserves careful attention. If the KPSS also fails to reject, both tests agree on stationarity. If it rejects stationarity, the series is near-integrated — behaving like a unit root process over some periods but stationary overall. The extensive literature on inflation persistence reflects precisely this ambiguity: inflation appears stationary over long samples but can exhibit near-unit-root behavior in particular episodes, especially the high-inflation 1970s.






NoteSummary of Integration Orders



Log GDP and log CPI are \(I(1)\): nonstationary in levels, stationary in first differences. GDP growth and inflation are \(I(0)\): stationary. This is a classic and well-established finding in macroeconometrics that motivates essentially all the model specifications we develop in this course.




Summary Statistics Table


Show code — Summary table
def summary_row(series, name, adf_reg, kpss_reg):
    clean     = series.dropna()
    adf_res   = adfuller(clean, regression=adf_reg, autolag="AIC")
    kpss_res  = kpss(clean, regression=kpss_reg, nlags="auto")
    adf_stars = ("***" if adf_res[1] < 0.01 else
                 "**"  if adf_res[1] < 0.05 else
                 "*"   if adf_res[1] < 0.10 else "")
    kpss_stars = ("***" if kpss_res[1] < 0.01 else
                  "**"  if kpss_res[1] < 0.05 else
                  "*"   if kpss_res[1] < 0.10 else "")
    return {
        "Series"       : name,
        "Obs"          : len(clean),
        "Mean"         : round(clean.mean(), 3),
        "Std. Dev."    : round(clean.std(),  3),
        "Min"          : round(clean.min(),  3),
        "Max"          : round(clean.max(),  3),
        "ADF Stat"     : f"{adf_res[0]:.3f}{adf_stars}",
        "ADF p-val"    : round(adf_res[1], 3),
        "KPSS Stat"    : f"{kpss_res[0]:.3f}{kpss_stars}",
        "KPSS p-val"   : round(kpss_res[1], 3),
    }

rows = [
    summary_row(gdp["Log Real GDP"],      "Log Real GDP",        "ct", "ct"),
    summary_row(gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"],  "GDP Growth (QoQ, %)", "c",  "c"),
    summary_row(cpi["Log CPI"],           "Log CPI",             "ct", "ct"),
    summary_row(cpi["Inflation (YoY)"],   "Inflation (YoY, %)","c",  "c"),
]

summary_df = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("Series")
print(summary_df.to_string())
print("\nADF: *** p<0.01  ** p<0.05  * p<0.10  (reject = stationary)")
print("KPSS: *** p<0.01  ** p<0.05  * p<0.10  (reject = unit root)")

Series

GDP Growth (QoQ, %)  257  0.735      1.064 -8.204   7.476  -15.364***      0.000    0.416*        0.07
Log CPI              774  4.702      0.747  3.380   5.747      -1.299      0.888   1.003**        0.01
Inflation (YoY, %)   762  3.697      2.669 -1.978  13.621    -3.199**      0.020   0.861**        0.01

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 2.2 Types of Datasets: Cross-Sectional, Time Series, and Panel Data
text:
2.2 Types of Datasets: Cross-Sectional, Time Series, and Panel Data
Economists work with three fundamental types of data, and keeping them distinct is essential for choosing the right methods.
Cross-sectional data capture many units at a single point in time. A survey of household incomes in 2023, a snapshot of firms’ balance sheets at the end of a fiscal year, the GDP of 150 countries in a given quarter — these are all cross-sectional. The organizing dimension is breadth: many observations of different units, all contemporaneous. The core statistical challenge is ensuring that units are representative of some population of interest, and that apparent relationships are not confounded by omitted factors. The standard assumption — sometimes heroic, but analytically necessary — is that observations are independent of each other.
Time series data follow a single unit — or a small number of units — across many points in time. US real GDP from 1947 to the present, the daily closing price of an equity index over ten years, the monthly unemployment rate for Germany since reunification — these are time series. The organizing dimension is depth: many observations of the same unit, ordered sequentially through time. The core statistical challenge is dependence: today’s observation is connected to yesterday’s, which was connected to the day before that. This dependence is not a flaw in the data; it is the feature we are trying to understand.






NoteDefinition 1.1 — Time Series



A time series is a collection of observations \(\{y_t\}_{t=1}^{T}\), where \(y_t\) denotes the value of the variable of interest at time index \(t\), and the index \(t\) runs from \(1\) to \(T\) in a fixed, ordered sequence. The subscript \(t\) is not a label — it encodes the temporal position of each observation and cannot be permuted without destroying the data’s structure.


Panel data (sometimes called longitudinal data) combine both dimensions: many units, each observed across many time periods. Following 50 US states over 30 years, tracking a sample of 10,000 workers over their careers, observing the quarterly earnings of 500 publicly traded firms across two decades — these are panel datasets. Their power lies in the combination: the time dimension allows researchers to control for unobserved unit-specific characteristics (fixed effects) that would confound a pure cross-sectional analysis, while the cross-sectional dimension provides variation that a pure time series cannot.






NoteDefinition 1.2 — Panel Data



A panel dataset is a collection of observations \(\{y_{it}\}\) where \(i = 1, \ldots, N\) indexes units and \(t = 1, \ldots, T\) indexes time periods. A balanced panel has \(N \times T\) observations. An unbalanced panel has gaps: some units are missing in some periods.


The critical difference for our purposes: in a panel, cross-sectional variation helps identify parameters that would be hard to pin down from a single series. In time series analysis, we must extract all information from the temporal variation of one — or a few — series. This constraint is not a weakness; it is a discipline that forces precision about the structure of the data-generating process. This course focuses on time series analysis; panel methods belong to a separate econometrics course.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
6.3 The Lag Operator
The most important notational device in time series analysis is the lag operator \(L\), defined by:
\[Ly_t = y_{t-1}\]
Applying it \(k\) times gives \(L^k y_t = y_{t-k}\). We also define \(L^{-1}y_t = y_{t+1}\) — the forward shift. A polynomial in \(L\),
\[\phi(L) = \phi_0 + \phi_1 L + \phi_2 L^2 + \cdots + \phi_p L^p\]
maps a time series to a linear combination of its current and past values:
\[\phi(L)y_t = \phi_0 y_t + \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \phi_2 y_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_p y_{t-p}\]
This compact notation becomes indispensable when writing time series models in Chapter 3.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
6.4 The Difference Operator
The first difference operator \(\Delta\) is:
\[\Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1} = (1 - L)y_t\]
The equality \(\Delta = 1 - L\) holds in the algebra of lag operators. Differencing removes a linear trend. The second difference \(\Delta^2 y_t = (1-L)^2 y_t = y_t - 2y_{t-1} + y_{t-2}\) removes a quadratic trend. In practice, one round of differencing is almost always sufficient for economic time series.
The seasonal difference operator removes seasonal patterns. For monthly data:
\[\Delta_{12} y_t = y_t - y_{t-12} = (1 - L^{12})y_t\]
compares each month to the same month a year earlier. For quarterly data, \(\Delta_4 y_t = y_t - y_{t-4}\) does the same.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 8.1 Time Series as Stochastic Difference Equations
text:
8.1 Time Series as Stochastic Difference Equations
This section is the mathematical core of the chapter. The machinery developed here — characteristic roots, companion matrices, eigenvalues, stability conditions — underpins every model in this course. It is worth working through carefully, because the payoff is large: by the end of this section, the concepts of stationarity, unit roots, and explosive processes follow almost as corollaries.
A time series model is, at its core, a difference equation — a rule relating the current value of a variable to its own past values and possibly to external inputs. A first-order deterministic example:
\[y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + c, \qquad t = 1, 2, 3, \ldots\]
where \(\phi\) and \(c\) are constants and \(y_0\) is a given initial condition. Adding white noise \(\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma^2)\):
\[y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + c + \varepsilon_t\]
gives a stochastic difference equation — and precisely the AR(1) model, one of the fundamental building blocks of time series econometrics. The mathematics of deterministic difference equations carries over directly to understanding the statistical properties of their stochastic counterparts. We develop the deterministic theory first.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
8.2 First-Order Linear Difference Equations

General Solution
The first-order linear difference equation with constant forcing term:
\[y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + c \tag{1.1}\]
We seek an explicit formula for \(y_t\) as a function of \(t\) and \(y_0\). The general solution decomposes as:
\[y_t = y_t^{(h)} + y_t^{(p)}\]
where \(y_t^{(h)}\) is the homogeneous solution and \(y_t^{(p)}\) is the particular solution.
Homogeneous solution. Setting \(c = 0\): we try \(y_t^{(h)} = A\lambda^t\). Substituting into \(y_t^{(h)} = \phi y_{t-1}^{(h)}\):
\[A\lambda^t = \phi A\lambda^{t-1} \implies \lambda = \phi\]
The homogeneous solution is \(y_t^{(h)} = A\phi^t\). The value \(\lambda = \phi\) is the characteristic root — the single root of the characteristic equation \(\lambda - \phi = 0\).
Particular solution. Trying a constant \(y_t^{(p)} = k\):
\[k = \phi k + c \implies k = \frac{c}{1-\phi}, \qquad \phi \neq 1\]
General solution. Applying the initial condition \(y_0\):
\[y_t = \left(y_0 - \frac{c}{1-\phi}\right)\phi^t + \frac{c}{1-\phi} \tag{1.2}\]
The solution is a weighted sum of the initial deviation from the long-run level \(\bar{y} = c/(1-\phi)\) — decaying at rate \(\phi^t\) — and the long-run level itself.


Stability and the Characteristic Root
The long-run behavior depends entirely on \(\phi\):






NoteDefinition 1.4 — Stability of a First-Order Difference Equation



The equation \(y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + c\) is:

Stable if \(|\phi| < 1\): \(y_t \to \bar{y} = c/(1-\phi)\) as \(t \to \infty\)
Unit root if \(|\phi| = 1\): shocks have permanent effects; the process is nonstationary
Explosive if \(|\phi| > 1\): deviations grow without bound

The long-run equilibrium \(\bar{y} = c/(1-\phi)\) exists only in the stable case.




The Unit Root Case
When \(\phi = 1\), the particular solution \(k = c/(1-\phi)\) does not exist. We try \(y_t^{(p)} = kt\):
\[kt = k(t-1) + c \implies k = c\]
The general solution when \(\phi = 1\) is \(y_t = y_0 + ct\) — growing linearly without bound when \(c \neq 0\) (random walk with drift), or remaining at \(y_0\) when \(c = 0\) (pure random walk).


The Stochastic Extension: AR(1)
Adding \(\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma^2)\) gives the AR(1) model:
\[y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + c + \varepsilon_t \tag{1.3}\]
The characteristic root \(\phi\) still governs behavior. When \(|\phi| < 1\), shocks are transitory — their influence decays geometrically at rate \(|\phi|^h\) after \(h\) periods. When \(\phi = 1\), every shock has a permanent, equal effect on the level of \(y_t\). This is the defining property of a unit root process, and it is the direct stochastic analogue of the deterministic analysis above.
The connection to white noise is direct and worth making explicit. When \(\phi = 1\), the model is \(y_t = y_{t-1} + \mu + \varepsilon_t\). Taking first differences:
\[\Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1} = \mu + \varepsilon_t\]
What remains after differencing is a constant plus a white noise process — nothing more. This is why first-differencing a unit root series achieves stationarity: it strips away the stochastic trend entirely, leaving behind the unpredictable innovation \(\varepsilon_t\) that was already present at each step. The random walk accumulates these innovations over time, which is what makes it nonstationary; first-differencing undoes that accumulation and recovers the white noise building block. The same logic extends to the general \(I(1)\) case: if \(y_t\) is integrated of order one, then \(\Delta y_t\) is stationary, and in the simplest case it reduces to white noise. This is the theoretical justification for the practical recommendation to difference before modeling.


Dynamic Regimes: A Visual Tour
The figure below illustrates the different dynamic regimes for a range of characteristic roots. All stable paths share the long-run equilibrium \(\bar{y} = 1.67\), and all paths begin at \(y_0 = 3\) — a positive deviation from \(\bar{y}\).


Show code — Figure 1.1
T    = 40
y0   = 3.0
ybar = 1.67

def simulate(phi, c, y0=y0, T=T):
    y = np.zeros(T)
    y[0] = y0
    for i in range(1, T):
        y[i] = phi * y[i-1] + c
    return y

panels = [
    ([(0.0,  0.5,          r"$\phi=0$",         EO_COPPER)],
     "(a) White noise around mean", True),
    ([(0.2,  0.5*(1-0.2),  r"$\phi=0.2$",       EO_COPPER),
      (0.7,  0.5*(1-0.7),  r"$\phi=0.7$",       EO_SAGE)],
     "(b) Stable, positive roots", True),
    ([(-0.2, 0.5*(1+0.2),  r"$\phi=-0.2$",      EO_COPPER),
      (-0.7, 0.5*(1+0.7),  r"$\phi=-0.7$",      EO_SAGE)],
     "(c) Stable, negative roots (oscillating)", True),
    ([(1.0,  0.0,          r"$\phi=1$",          EO_SKYBLUE),
      (-1.0, 0.0,          r"$\phi=-1$",         EO_LAVENDER)],
     "(d) Unit roots, no drift ($c=0$)", False),
    ([(1.0,  0.5,          r"$\phi=1$, drift",   EO_SKYBLUE),
      (-1.0, 0.5,          r"$\phi=-1$, drift",  EO_LAVENDER)],
     r"(e) Unit roots, positive drift ($c=0.5$)", False),
    ([(1.2,  0.0,          r"$\phi=1.2$",        EO_TERRACOTTA)],
     "(f) Explosive, positive root", False),
    ([(-1.2, 0.0,          r"$\phi=-1.2$",       EO_TERRACOTTA)],
     "(g) Explosive, negative root (oscillating)", False),
]

T_exp = 12
fig, axes = plt.subplots(len(panels), 1, figsize=(6, 14))

for ax, (specs, title, show_ybar) in zip(axes, panels):
    is_exp = "(f)" in title or "(g)" in title
    T_use  = T_exp if is_exp else T
    t_use  = np.arange(T_use)
    for phi, c, lab, col in specs:
        y = simulate(phi, c, T=T_use)
        ax.plot(t_use, y, color=col, label=lab, linewidth=1.2)
    if show_ybar:
        ax.axhline(ybar, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.7,
                   linestyle=":", alpha=0.6, label=r"$\bar{y}=1.67$")
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel("Time $t$")
    ax.set_ylabel("$y_t$")
    ax.legend(fontsize=6, loc="upper right")
    eo_style_ax(ax)

eo_suptitle(fig, "Dynamic Regimes of the First-Order Difference Equation")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 8.1: Solution paths of the first-order difference equation \(y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + c\) for eight characteristic roots, all starting at \(y_0 = 3\). Stable panels (a–c) show convergence to \(\bar{y} = 1.67\) (dotted line). Positive roots produce monotone paths; negative roots produce oscillation. Unit root paths (d) with \(c=0\) wander without returning to any level; with \(c=0.5\) (e) they trend linearly. Explosive paths (f–g) diverge rapidly; the negative explosive root produces amplifying oscillation.





Several features are worth noting. In panels (a) and (b), positive characteristic roots produce monotone convergence: the path moves smoothly toward \(\bar{y}\) without overshooting. In panel (c), negative roots produce oscillating convergence: the path alternates above and below \(\bar{y}\) while converging — a stylized representation of the kind of fluctuation we see in some economic series. Panels (d) and (e) show unit root behavior: without drift the path wanders aimlessly; with positive drift it trends steadily upward, never returning to any fixed level. Panels (f) and (g) show explosive behavior over a shorter horizon, illustrating how rapidly the process diverges when \(|\phi| > 1\).
An important distinction: the oscillation produced by a negative real root in panel (c) and (g) alternates every single period — the sign of \(y_t - \bar{y}\) flips at each step because \((-|\phi|)^t\) alternates in sign. This period-2 oscillation is the fastest possible. We will see shortly that complex roots produce smoother oscillations with longer periods, more representative of actual business cycles. The two mechanisms look superficially similar — both oscillate — but differ fundamentally in their period and in the functional form of the solution.


The Random Walk: A Special Case Worth Seeing
The pure random walk (\(\phi = 1\), \(c = 0\)) deserves its own figure. Unlike any stable process, different realizations of a random walk fan out in completely different directions from the same starting point, and none of them returns to the initial level. The figure below simulates twenty independent random walk paths from the same starting value — the fan-shaped spreading is the visual signature of a unit root.


Show code — Figure 1.2
rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=42)
T   = 100
N   = 20    # number of paths

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3.5))

for i in range(N):
    eps  = rng.normal(0, 1, T)
    path = np.cumsum(eps)
    path = np.insert(path, 0, 0)
    ax.plot(np.arange(T+1), path, color=EO_COPPER,
            linewidth=0.6, alpha=0.4)

# Theoretical +/- 2*sqrt(t) envelope
t_vec = np.arange(1, T+1)
ax.fill_between(t_vec,  2*np.sqrt(t_vec),
                        -2*np.sqrt(t_vec),
                color=EO_SKYBLUE, alpha=0.12,
                label=r"$\pm 2\sqrt{t}$ envelope")
ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.7,
           linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
ax.set_title("Twenty Realizations of a Random Walk")
ax.set_xlabel("Time $t$")
ax.set_ylabel("$y_t$")
ax.legend(fontsize=6)
eo_style_ax(ax)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 8.2: Twenty simulated random walk paths from the same starting value \(y_0 = 0\). Each path is a single realization of \(y_t = y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t\) with \(\varepsilon_t \sim N(0,1)\). Unlike a stable process, the paths do not return to any fixed level — they spread out indefinitely. The grey band shows the theoretical \(\pm 2\sqrt{t}\) envelope, confirming that the standard deviation grows as \(\sqrt{t}\).





The grey band shows the theoretical \(\pm 2\sqrt{t}\) envelope — the region within which each path falls with approximately 95% probability at each point in time. The envelope widens as \(t\) grows, confirming the key property: \(\text{Var}(y_t) = t\sigma^2\) grows linearly with time. No stable process has this property.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 8.3 Higher-Order Difference Equations, the Companion Matrix, and Eigenvalues
text:
8.3 Higher-Order Difference Equations, the Companion Matrix, and Eigenvalues

The \(p\)-th Order Case
A \(p\)-th order linear difference equation with a constant:
\[y_t = \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \phi_2 y_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_p y_{t-p} + c \tag{1.4}\]
Using the lag operator: \(\Phi(L)\,y_t = c\) where \(\Phi(L) = 1 - \phi_1 L - \cdots - \phi_p L^p\). Adding white noise gives the AR(\(p\)) model:
\[y_t = \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \cdots + \phi_p y_{t-p} + c + \varepsilon_t \tag{1.5}\]
The AR(\(p\)) is the direct multiperiod generalization of the AR(1): the current value depends on the previous \(p\) periods, each with its own coefficient.


The Companion Matrix Representation
A key insight — connecting difference equations to linear algebra — is that any \(p\)-th order scalar difference equation can be rewritten as a first-order vector difference equation by stacking the relevant lags into a state vector.
Define the \(p\)-dimensional state vector \(\mathbf{z}_t = (y_t,\, y_{t-1},\, \ldots,\, y_{t-p+1})'\). Then equation (1.4) can be written as:
\[\mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{F}\mathbf{z}_{t-1} + \mathbf{c} \tag{1.6}\]
where \(\mathbf{F}\) is the \(p \times p\) companion matrix:
\[\mathbf{F} = \begin{pmatrix}
\phi_1 & \phi_2 & \phi_3 & \cdots & \phi_{p-1} & \phi_p \\
1      & 0      & 0      & \cdots & 0          & 0      \\
0      & 1      & 0      & \cdots & 0          & 0      \\
\vdots &        & \ddots &        &            & \vdots \\
0      & 0      & 0      & \cdots & 1          & 0
\end{pmatrix}\]
and \(\mathbf{c} = (c, 0, 0, \ldots, 0)'\). The companion matrix has the AR coefficients in its first row, and an identity submatrix in the remaining rows that simply records that \(y_{t-j} = y_{t-j}\).
To verify: reading the first row of (1.6) gives \(y_t = \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \phi_2 y_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_p y_{t-p} + c\) — exactly equation (1.4). Reading the second row gives \(y_{t-1} = y_{t-1}\) — trivially true. The companion matrix representation is not a new equation; it is the original \(p\)-th order equation reorganized.


Eigenvalues and Stability
The solution of the first-order vector system (1.6) depends on the powers of \(\mathbf{F}\): iterating backwards, \(\mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{F}^t \mathbf{z}_0 + \sum_{s=0}^{t-1}\mathbf{F}^s \mathbf{c}\). The behavior of \(\mathbf{F}^t\) as \(t \to \infty\) is determined by the eigenvalues of \(\mathbf{F}\) — the values \(\lambda\) satisfying \(\det(\mathbf{F} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0\). Recall from linear algebra that eigenvalues are the scalars by which a matrix stretches or contracts its eigenvectors; when we raise a matrix to a power \(t\), the eigenvalues are raised to that same power \(\lambda^t\), so their moduli determine whether the system grows, decays, or oscillates as \(t\) increases.
A fundamental result of linear algebra connects the eigenvalues of \(\mathbf{F}\) directly to the characteristic roots of the original scalar equation:






NoteProposition 1.1 — Eigenvalues of the Companion Matrix



The eigenvalues of the companion matrix \(\mathbf{F}\) are identical to the characteristic roots \(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_p\) of the \(p\)-th order difference equation (1.4).


To see why, note that the characteristic polynomial of \(\mathbf{F}\) — the polynomial whose roots are its eigenvalues — is:
\[\det(\lambda\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{F}) = \lambda^p - \phi_1\lambda^{p-1} - \phi_2\lambda^{p-2} - \cdots - \phi_p\]
which is exactly the characteristic equation of the scalar difference equation. The eigenvalues of \(\mathbf{F}\) and the characteristic roots of (1.4) are the same objects.
This connection is not merely algebraic — it is conceptually important. It means the stability of a time series process can be analyzed entirely through the eigenvalues of its companion matrix, a standard object in linear algebra. For an AR(2) with \(p = 2\), for example:
\[\mathbf{F} = \begin{pmatrix} \phi_1 & \phi_2 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}\]
The eigenvalues are the two roots of \(\lambda^2 - \phi_1\lambda - \phi_2 = 0\) — precisely the characteristic equation. Whether these eigenvalues lie inside, on, or outside the unit circle determines whether the AR(2) process is stable, has a unit root, or is explosive.






NoteDefinition 1.5 — Stability of a \(p\)-th Order Difference Equation



The equation (1.4) — equivalently, the AR(\(p\)) model (1.5) — is stable if and only if all \(p\) eigenvalues of the companion matrix \(\mathbf{F}\) satisfy \(|\lambda_j| < 1\). Equivalently, all roots of the characteristic polynomial \(\lambda^p - \phi_1\lambda^{p-1} - \cdots - \phi_p = 0\) lie strictly inside the unit circle.
If any eigenvalue has \(|\lambda_j| = 1\), the system has a unit root. If any eigenvalue has \(|\lambda_j| > 1\), the system is explosive.


The general homogeneous solution, when all eigenvalues are distinct, is:
\[y_t^{(h)} = A_1\lambda_1^t + A_2\lambda_2^t + \cdots + A_p\lambda_p^t\]
where \(A_1, \ldots, A_p\) are determined by \(p\) initial conditions. The particular solution for a constant forcing term, when no eigenvalue equals 1, is:
\[\bar{y} = \frac{c}{1 - \phi_1 - \phi_2 - \cdots - \phi_p} = \frac{c}{\Phi(1)}\]






WarningTwo Equivalent Stationarity Conventions



In terms of the characteristic equation: all eigenvalues satisfy \(|\lambda_j| < 1\) (inside the unit circle). In terms of the lag polynomial \(\Phi(z) = 1 - \phi_1 z - \cdots - \phi_p z^p\): all roots of \(\Phi(z) = 0\) satisfy \(|z_j| > 1\) (outside the unit circle). These are equivalent because the roots of \(\Phi(z)\) are the reciprocals of the eigenvalues.




Real vs. Complex Eigenvalues and Oscillatory Behavior
When all eigenvalues are real, the solution path is monotone (positive eigenvalue) or alternating in sign (negative eigenvalue) at each period, as we saw in the first-order case. When eigenvalues are complex — always occurring in conjugate pairs \(\lambda, \bar{\lambda} = re^{\pm i\omega}\) for equations with real coefficients — the solution exhibits oscillatory behavior of a different and richer kind.
To understand this intuitively, a complex eigenvalue pair encodes two pieces of information simultaneously: how quickly the oscillation decays or grows (the modulus \(r = |\lambda|\)) and how fast the oscillation cycles (the argument \(\omega\)). The contribution of a complex conjugate pair to the solution is a damped sinusoid:
\[A\,r^t\cos(\omega t + \theta)\]
where \(A\) and \(\theta\) are determined by initial conditions. The modulus \(r\) is the envelope:

When \(r < 1\): the amplitude \(r^t\) shrinks — a damped oscillation that gradually returns to the long-run level. This is the pattern associated with stable business cycle dynamics.
When \(r = 1\): a sustained oscillation that neither grows nor decays.
When \(r > 1\): an amplifying oscillation — explosive in the oscillatory sense.

The argument \(\omega\) (in radians per period) determines the period of the cycle: \(P = 2\pi/\omega\) periods to complete one full oscillation. A complex eigenvalue pair with \(\omega = \pi/6\) and quarterly data implies a cycle period of \(P = 2\pi/(\pi/6) = 12\) quarters — a three-year cycle, squarely in the range of typical business cycle frequencies.
This is the key distinction from negative real eigenvalues. A negative real eigenvalue \(\lambda < 0\) produces oscillation that alternates every single period: \(\lambda^t\) changes sign at each step because \((-|\lambda|)^t\) alternates. This is period-2 oscillation — the fastest possible, completing a full cycle in just two periods. Complex eigenvalues, by contrast, allow any period \(P = 2\pi/\omega \geq 2\). Real economies exhibit business cycles with periods of 6–32 quarters — far too slow to be explained by negative real eigenvalues, but naturally produced by complex conjugate pairs with appropriate arguments. The oscillatory behavior of complex roots is therefore not just a mathematical curiosity; it is one of the mechanisms through which business-cycle-like dynamics arise in AR(\(p\)) models.


Show code — Figure 1.3
def ar2_from_complex_roots(r, omega, T=60, y0=2.0, y1=1.5):
    phi1 =  2 * r * np.cos(omega)
    phi2 = -r ** 2
    y    = np.zeros(T)
    y[0] = y0
    y[1] = y1
    for t in range(2, T):
        y[t] = phi1 * y[t-1] + phi2 * y[t-2]
    return y

omega  = np.pi / 6
specs  = [(0.85, EO_COPPER,     r"Damped ($r=0.85$)",         60),
          (1.00, EO_SKYBLUE,    r"Unit modulus ($r=1.00$)",   60),
          (1.10, EO_TERRACOTTA, r"Explosive ($r=1.10$)",      30)]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(6, 7))

for ax, (r, col, lab, T_use) in zip(axes, specs):
    y = ar2_from_complex_roots(r, omega, T=T_use)
    ax.plot(np.arange(T_use), y, color=col, linewidth=1.2, label=lab)
    ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.5,
               linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
    ax.set_title(lab)
    ax.set_xlabel("Time $t$")
    ax.set_ylabel("$y_t$")
    eo_style_ax(ax)

eo_suptitle(fig, "Oscillatory Behavior of Complex Eigenvalues")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 8.3: AR(2) solution paths for complex eigenvalues with the same argument \(\omega = \pi/6\) (twelve-period cycle, three years of quarterly data) but different moduli. The damped case (\(r=0.85\), copper) oscillates and converges. The unit-modulus case (\(r=1.0\), blue) sustains the oscillation indefinitely. The explosive case (\(r=1.1\), terracotta) shows growing oscillations. Note the smooth sinusoidal shape — quite different from the period-2 alternation produced by negative real eigenvalues.







Root Diagrams
A convenient visual summary of the stability condition plots the eigenvalues in the complex plane with the unit circle as a reference. All eigenvalues inside the circle — stable. Any eigenvalue on the circle — unit root. Any eigenvalue outside — explosive.


Show code — Figure 1.4
def char_roots_ar2(phi1, phi2):
    return np.roots([1, -phi1, -phi2])

specs_rd = [
    (0.5,  0.3,   "Stable"),
    (1.0,  0.0,   "Unit root"),
    (0.3, -1.1,   "Explosive"),
]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(6, 2.8))
theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 300)

for ax, (phi1, phi2, title) in zip(axes, specs_rd):
    roots = char_roots_ar2(phi1, phi2)
    ax.plot(np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta),
            color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.7, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
    ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.3)
    ax.axvline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.3)
    for root in roots:
        ax.scatter(root.real, root.imag,
                   color=EO_COPPER, s=35, zorder=5)
    ax.set_xlim(-1.6, 1.6)
    ax.set_ylim(-1.4, 1.4)
    ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel("Real part")
    ax.set_ylabel("Imag. part")
    ax.grid(False)
    eo_style_ax(ax)

eo_suptitle(fig, "Eigenvalues and the Unit Circle")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 8.4: Eigenvalue diagrams for three AR(2) specifications. Left: both roots inside the unit circle — stable, stationary process (\(\phi_1=0.5\), \(\phi_2=0.3\); roots at \(0.85\) and \(-0.35\)). Center: one root exactly on the circle — unit root (\(\phi_1=1.0\), \(\phi_2=0.0\); roots at \(1.0\) and \(0.0\)). Right: both complex roots outside the unit circle — explosive process (\(\phi_1=0.3\), \(\phi_2=-1.1\); complex conjugate pair with modulus \(1.05\)).
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
10.2 Strict and Weak Stationarity






NoteDefinition 1.6 — Strict Stationarity



A stochastic process \(\{y_t\}\) is strictly stationary if, for any finite collection of time indices \(t_1 < t_2 < \cdots < t_k\) and any integer \(h\), the joint distribution of \((y_{t_1}, \ldots, y_{t_k})\) is identical to the joint distribution of \((y_{t_1+h}, \ldots, y_{t_k+h})\).


Strict stationarity says that shifting the entire process by any number of periods changes nothing about its distribution — not means, not variances, not shapes, not tails. This is an extremely strong condition, rarely verified directly. In practice we rely on a weaker but more tractable definition.






NoteDefinition 1.7 — Weak (Covariance) Stationarity



A stochastic process \(\{y_t\}\) is weakly stationary (or covariance stationary) if:

\(\mathbb{E}[y_t] = \mu < \infty\) for all \(t\) — the mean is finite and constant over time.
\(\text{Var}(y_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty\) for all \(t\) — the variance is finite and constant over time.
\(\text{Cov}(y_t, y_{t-h}) = \gamma(h)\) for all \(t\) and all \(h\) — the covariance between any two observations depends only on the lag \(h\), not on when in time they occur.



Condition 1 rules out trending means. A series whose average level grows over time — like the level of nominal GDP — fails this condition immediately.
Condition 2 rules out changing variance. As we saw in the random walk figure, \(\text{Var}(y_t)\) grows linearly with \(t\) for a unit root process — a direct violation.
Condition 3 is the subtlest. It says the covariance structure depends only on distance in time, not on location in time. The covariance between GDP growth in 1995Q1 and 1995Q2 should equal the covariance between GDP growth in 2010Q1 and 2010Q2 — both pairs are one quarter apart. This is what makes the autocovariance function \(\gamma(h)\) a well-defined, estimable object: we pool all pairs of observations separated by lag \(h\), regardless of when they occur, to estimate a single number.
The relationship between the two definitions: strict stationarity with finite variance implies weak stationarity. The converse is false in general. The important exception is Gaussian processes — because the normal distribution is fully characterized by its first two moments, a weakly stationary Gaussian process is automatically strictly stationary. Weak stationarity is the operative assumption throughout this course.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
10.3 Types of Non-Stationarity
Not all non-stationarity is the same, and the type matters for how we handle it. There are three main forms in economic data.

Trend Stationarity
A process is trend stationary if it fluctuates around a deterministic trend — a fixed function of time — with stationary fluctuations:
\[y_t = \alpha + \beta t + u_t\]
where \(u_t\) is weakly stationary. The series \(y_t\) is nonstationary because \(\mathbb{E}[y_t] = \alpha + \beta t\) depends on \(t\). But the deviations from trend are stationary. Crucially, a shock that perturbs \(u_t\) at time \(t\) has only a transitory effect: the series eventually returns to the deterministic trend path. To achieve stationarity, we detrend — regress \(y_t\) on a constant and time and work with the residuals.


Difference Stationarity and Unit Roots
A process is difference stationary if it is nonstationary in levels but stationary after first differencing — arising when the characteristic polynomial has a root on the unit circle. The canonical example is the random walk with drift:
\[y_t = \mu + y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t\]
First differencing gives \(\Delta y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t\), which is stationary. But the level is not: its variance grows without bound, and unlike the trend-stationary case, shocks are permanent — a positive \(\varepsilon_t\) raises \(y_t\) permanently above the path it would otherwise have followed.
The distinction between transitory and permanent shocks has profound economic implications. Is an unexpected recession a transitory deviation from a stable growth path, or a permanent downward shift in the level of output? The answer changes the interpretation of business cycles, the conduct of policy, and the specification of macroeconometric models.
A series requiring \(d\) rounds of differencing to achieve stationarity is integrated of order \(d\), written \(I(d)\). Most economic level series are \(I(1)\).


Explosive Processes
When any eigenvalue has modulus strictly greater than 1, the process is explosive: deviations grow without bound. Explosive processes are rare in economic data over long horizons, but arise in specific contexts — asset price bubbles being the most prominent example. Detecting such episodes is an active research area that builds directly on the methods developed here.






NoteSummary: Types of Non-Stationarity






Table 10.1: Three types of non-stationarity and their treatments











Type
Source
Shocks
Fix




Trend stationary
